comment.body: The trump tweet posted above could imply that the notion of "tariffs will be eased" is wrong and could start another leg down. But I wouldn't be surprised if we start seeing things like "this is a one time thing...tarriff inflation is transitory" yaddah yaddah as we move down. So I think, retest of a low here into May OPEX but then itll get really choppy until the Fed meeting

comment.body:
Indeed, I remember Gerhard Schroeder's era. It is totally understandable that Eastern European nations are traumatized by Russian imperialism. Yet, neither the war in Abkhazia and South Ossetia in 2008 nor the Crimea crisis in 2014 were escalated beyond regional level in the way the 2022 war was; there are likely miscalculations and misjudgments. As Alma wrote, these things are not purely black and white; the judgement is best left to God... 

If we look forward, it's more important to see how we navigate in the woods and clean up the mess... We have very much sleepwalked into Cold War 2.0, except we no longer have Churchill's Iron Curtain, and China, integrated into the global economy, in some sense is a more dangerous and capable adversary...

comment.body:
I don't think Ukraine and Russia will reach a ceasefire agreement. Of course, in reality, they don't necessarily fight battles every single day — it’s more like occasionally throwing a bomb here and there. Either way, the war of attrition will eventually wear Ukraine down.
Europe, on the surface, has to show support for Ukraine, especially Germany, which is happily rearming itself. Germany’s military-industrial strength is very powerful, but historically, their arms orders were suppressed by the United States, because within NATO, the U.S. paid the bill and thus had the leverage to prioritize selling their own weapons to the world.
Moreover, Putin has absolutely no respect for Trump or people like Witkoff. Americans say Witkoff was just someone who used to collect rent with a gun. Putin sees himself as an emperor, while Trump is merely a businessman — they are not in the same class. Putin certainly doesn’t need to outright reject America; he can just find some excuse to make negotiations fall through.
In the meantime, Putin will slowly nibble away at Ukraine. Ukraine is already in ruins, and as time passes, when people realize there’s no hope of rebuilding their homes, they’ll move away. In a deserted, uninhabited land, who would stay to defend it?

Germany’s military-industrial strength is very powerful, but historically, their arms orders were suppressed by the United States, because within NATO, the U.S. paid the bill and thus had the leverage to prioritize selling their own weapons to the world.
Moreover, Putin has absolutely no respect for Trump or people like Witkoff. Americans say Witkoff was just someone who used to collect rent with a gun. Putin sees himself as an emperor, while Trump is merely a businessman — they are not in the same class. Putin certainly doesn’t need to outright reject America; he can just find some excuse to make negotiations fall through.
In the meantime, Putin will slowly nibble away at Ukraine. Ukraine is already in ruins, and as time passes, when people realize there’s no hope of rebuilding their homes, they’ll move away. In a deserted, uninhabited land, who would stay to defend it?

comment.body:
In May, I expect Trump’s report card to be basically zero, and then he’ll start ranting and raving on Truth Social again. He simply doesn’t have the class or stature.
In mid-May, he's planning to visit the Middle East, but I don't think the Middle Eastern monarchs will give him any real respect.
China, Russia, and the Middle East — these countries are all built on imperial systems. In the past, the U.S. was able to counterbalance them largely through its "soft power."
Now, with someone like Trump barging onto the scene, of course they’re happy to see America's strength weakened — but that doesn’t mean they will show Trump any real respect. They simply aren’t in the same class.

comment.body: Trump does enjoy good relations with the Gulf monarchies, whose thrones and security depend on the US. In Trump's first term, his administration helped Crown Prince MBS (who took power via a coup) become the de-facto leader of Saudi Arabia. The Arabs are pragmatists, and as Alma wrote, they have always been hedged by staying largely neutral among the US, Russia and China; they will only bet on the US if they have the confidence; this is the way how they are.

comment.body:
Here's a structured analysis based on the information provided and current economic trends:

---

### 1. **China Shipment Disruptions**
- **Immediate Collapse**: A near-stop in shipments within two weeks aligns with escalations in trade tensions (e.g., new tariffs, export controls). Recent U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs, steel, and semiconductors (May 2024) could explain abrupt supply-chain pullbacks.
- **Supply-Chain Chaos**: Ships returning mid-voyage suggest importers are rushing to avoid tariffs or preempt embargoes. This risks port congestion, contract disputes, and inventory shortfalls.

---

### 2. **Consumer Impact**
- **Short-Term Price Spikes**: Electronics, auto parts, and holiday goods (e.g., back-to-school inventory) may see immediate shortages, driving up prices.
- **Long-Term Inflation**: If tariffs persist, retailers will pass costs to consumers. The U.S. imports ~18% of goods from China; sectors like apparel and machinery are especially vulnerable.

---

### 3. **Stock Market Reactions**
- **Transportation Sector**: Companies like ZIM, Matson, and port operators (e.g., SSA Marine) could plummet on volume declines. Shipping rates (Freightos Index) may drop short-term but rebound if capacity is cut.
- **Retail and Manufacturing**: Target, Dollar General, and auto manufacturers relying on Chinese components (e.g., Tesla) may warn of margin pressures, triggering sell-offs.
- **Winners**: Domestic manufacturers (e.g., US Steel) and alternative sourcing hubs (Vietnam, India) could rally on substitution demand.

---

### 4. **News Cycle Timeline**
- **Week 1**: Trade journalists and logistics firms (e.g., Flexport, FreightWaves) will report unusual ship movements. 
- **Week 2**: Mainstream outlets (WSJ, Bloomberg) will link the halt to policy leaks or negotiations. Expect mentions in Fed speeches or White House briefings.
- **Week 3-4**: Earnings pre-announcements from affected companies; possible White House tariff exemptions to calm markets.

---

### 5. **Key Triggers to Monitor**
- **China’s Retaliation**: Export bans on rare earths (used in EVs/tech) or agricultural purchases (soybeans) would escalate tensions.
- **CPI Data**: June/July inflation prints may spike, forcing the Fed to delay rate cuts.
- **Corporate Guidance**: Walmart, Home Depot, and Best Buy may issue warnings in late July earnings calls.

---

**Actionable Takeaway**: Prepare for volatility in consumer discretionary stocks and container-shipping ETFs (e.g., SEA). Hedge with inflation-protected securities (TIPS) and domestic industrial equities. Monitor real-time AIS shipping data (via MarineTraffic) for confirmation of the trend.

comment.body:
Interesting comments by Rubio on Meet The Press this morning referring to Ukraine/Russia esp considering the meeting with Trump and EU leaders at the Pope's funeral:

"This week is going to be a really important week in which we (US) have to make a determination about whether this is an endeavor that we want to continue to be involved in or its time to sort of focus on some other issues that are equally or more important in some cases..."

"We're close but not close enough"

"There are reasons to be optimistic and there are reasons to be concerned"

"This can get worse in months to come"
comment.raw_body:
Interesting comments by Rubio on Meet The Press this morning referring to Ukraine/Russia esp considering the meeting with Trump and EU leaders at the Pope's funeral:

"This week is going to be a really important week in which we (US) have to make a determination about whether this is an endeavor that we want to continue to be involved in or its time to sort of focus on some other issues that are equally or more important in some cases..."

"We're close but not close enough"

"There are reasons to be optimistic and there are reasons to be concerned"

"This can get worse in months to come"

comment.body:
Bessent sounding a bit defensive in this interview on the the market, employment and tariffs.  Trying to read the tea leaves between him and Rubio, and I don't get a whole lot of confidence about direction on all the issues that are ongoing.

comment.body:
Hi Alma, thank you for the wonderful writing today. It helped me a lot to understand the geo impact on the market

I'm curious though why you are calling for another big leg down as far as SPX 4000, even if negotiations are going well?
We're currently (potentially) in a short squeeze that seems to be also driven by some good geo news (trump / russia negotiation). If mid-May meetings all go well, then isn't it possible we just continue to ride up as the market gets news tariffs would be rolled back?
comment.raw_body:
